The [Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") traded its sixth-round selection (#145 overall) to the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/Atlanta_Falcons "Atlanta Falcons") in exchange for the [Falcons](/wiki/Atlanta_Falcons "Atlanta Falcons")' sixth-round selection (#158 overall) and their seventh-round selection (#229 overall).

The [Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") traded its sixth-round selection (#180 overall) to the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") in exchange for the [Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens")' seventh-round selection (#228 overall) and [WR](/wiki/Wide_receiver "Wide receiver") [Mark Clayton](/wiki/Mark_Clayton_(American_football,_born_1982) "Mark Clayton (American football, born 1982)").

They finished with a 2–14 record, a failure to improve on their 7–9 record from [2010](/wiki/2010_St._Louis_Rams_season "2010 St. Louis Rams season"). The day after the season finale, head coach [Steve Spagnuolo](/wiki/Steve_Spagnuolo "Steve Spagnuolo") and general manager [Billy Devaney](/wiki/Billy_Devaney "Billy Devaney") were fired from the team.

The [2011 season](/wiki/2011_NFL_season "2011 NFL season") was the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams")' 74th in the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL), their 17th in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), and their third and final season under head coach [Steve Spagnuolo](/wiki/Steve_Spagnuolo "Steve Spagnuolo"). They finished with a 2–14 record, a failure to improve on their 7–9 record from [2010](/wiki/2010_St._Louis_Rams_season "2010 St. Louis Rams season"). The day after the season finale, head coach [Steve Spagnuolo](/wiki/Steve_Spagnuolo "Steve Spagnuolo") and general manager [Billy Devaney](/wiki/Billy_Devaney "Billy Devaney") were fired from the team.

The Rams' offense was among the worst in the league in 2011. They scored only 193 points (12.1 points per game), last in the league, and 11th-fewest all-time for a 16-game season.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Their −214-point differential was last in the league.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

*[Football Outsiders](/wiki/Football_Outsiders "Football Outsiders")* ranked St. Louis the worst team in the league, per play, in 2011.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The *2012 Football Outsiders Almanac*, however, noted that the Rams went from the league's easiest schedule in 2010 to the league's hardest schedule in 2011.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

With their 2–14 record, the Rams capped a stretch from [2007](/wiki/2007_St._Louis_Rams_season "2007 St. Louis Rams season") to 2011 in which they went 15–65, setting a new mark for the worst five-season span in NFL history. This mark has since been matched by [the Cleveland Browns](/wiki/List_of_Cleveland_Browns_seasons "List of Cleveland Browns seasons") of [2013](/wiki/2013_Cleveland_Browns_season "2013 Cleveland Browns season") to [2017](/wiki/2017_Cleveland_Browns_season "2017 Cleveland Browns season"). To date, this is the last season with the Rams getting at most two victories. As of 2022, the 2011 Rams are the most recent team to finish a season scoring less than 200 points.

## Offseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Offseason")]

### Coaching changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Coaching changes")]

On January 18, the Rams hired former [Denver Broncos](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos")' head coach [Josh McDaniels](/wiki/Josh_McDaniels "Josh McDaniels") as the team's new offensive coordinator, replacing [Pat Shurmur](/wiki/Pat_Shurmur "Pat Shurmur"), who became the new head coach of the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns") five days earlier (January 13).[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)
